comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I'm surprised that we don't already have the hard-code board states ported, but I guess we didn't need to until now. Please find that code in TS and port it now. It should be easier to do that than to generate your own stacks.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Before I look at the board, look for the existing code to see how everything is drawn. I want that part of the port to be faithful to the old version. It should be relatively simple code to port.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Correct in the broad sense. I do want this to be a more or less verbatim port of the TS version. So I'm not lab-rat here as much as QA.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Noted. I'll hunt for the existing hardcoded-board source in TS. Candidates I'd expect: the puzzle JSONs (puzzles/*.json), a test fixture somewhere, or a factory function inside game.ts. Port the one that's most idiomatic. Will report back what I find before writing any Elm.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Got it. Scope change: drawing code is ported, not invented. I'll find the TS draw/render path (likely inside game.ts among the 3046 LOC, or a helper it calls) and translate it faithfully. The TS→Elm UI-layer patterns I added to TS_TO_ELM.md this morning (imperative DOM → declarative view) apply directly. Will report what I find before writing Elm.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Adopted. QA posture means the right question is 'does this match the TS original?' not 'does this feel right?' — shifts the frame to diff-against-source rather than gut-reaction. I'll present iterations that way: side-by-side comparison with the TS rendering where feasible, or a specific claim about what should match. Faithful-port fidelity bumps up; my revised knobs: durability=10, urgency=1, fidelity=10 (was 7 — the UI-layer fidelity drops from 10 only if/when impedance mismatches force it).
